hypothesis: The bucketed two-stage soft-codebook family that now wins at K=16, K=24, and K=32 under EF=16 may also extend its advantage into the next higher anchor at K=64, revealing whether its locality bias is a broad backbone improvement rather than only a low-K effect.
summary: After three consecutive frontier improvements at K=16, K=24, and K=32, the highest-value next step is to test the same family at K=64 with the recipe held fixed. This changes only K, respects the fixed EF=16 policy, and directly measures how far the bucketed family’s gain extends along the Pareto curve.
self_review: This is the right continuation after a contiguous run of wins: keep the architecture and training recipe fixed and map the next frontier-relevant K. The main risk is that the bucketed inductive bias may stop helping at higher K where global competition becomes more valuable, but K=64 is the cleanest way to test that boundary.
notes_to_memory: Round 128 continues frontier mapping of the bucketed family after wins at K=16, K=24, and K=32 under EF=16.
Hold architecture and recipe fixed so the result isolates whether the bucketed gain extends to the higher-K lane.
A strong K=64 result would indicate the bucketed family is a broad EF=16 backbone candidate rather than only a low-K specialist.
next_hypotheses: If K=64 is competitive, test K=96 next to map whether the bucketed family can challenge the high-K routed anchor under EF=16.
If K=64 regresses meaningfully while K<=32 remain strong, treat the bucketed family as a low-to-mid-K specialist and prioritize confirmation or optimization within that band.
If K=64 is close but not clearly better, run one confirmatory repeat before promoting the family as the default EF=16 backbone above K=32.
